whoUses: everyone online, all ages by now, originally younger internet users
usageContext: Originated from repeated w (from 笑う, warau, 'to laugh') stacking up — wwwww — until it visually resembled blades of grass; 草 became a shorthand for the same effect. 大草原 (daisougen, 'huge field of grass') escalates it for something extremely funny.

natural: an all-purpose intensifier — can mean amazing, terrible, or intense depending purely on tone/context
whoUses: everyone informally, all ages, nationwide — one of the most frequent words in casual spoken Japanese
usageContext: Context alone decides the polarity: やばい, おいしい！ ('this is amazingly good!') vs. やばい、遅刻する！ ('crap, I'm going to be late!'). Impossible to translate with one fixed English word — the versatility itself is the point.

literal: shortened from 本気 (honki, 'serious') read as がち in a wrestling-slang sense
whoUses: younger speakers especially, but broadly understood nationwide
usageContext: The current go-to intensifier for younger speakers, doing the job マジ (maji) did a generation earlier: ガチで (gachi de, 'seriously'), ガチ勢 (gachizei, 'people who take [a hobby] seriously').

literal: from English 'emotional', adjective-ified with the Japanese い-ending
natural: emotionally moving, often with a nostalgic or bittersweet edge
whoUses: younger speakers, nationwide, common online and in casual speech
usageContext: Describes anything that stirs up feeling without needing to specify which feeling — a sunset, an old song, a graduation photo. Deliberately vaguer than 感動的 (kandouteki, 'moving/touching'), which sounds more formal and specific.

examples.reading: このしゃしん、エモいね。

natural: a cutesy, often half-joking 'sad face' — more performative pouting than real sadness
whoUses: mostly young women and teens, nationwide, very online
usageContext: Usually paired with a specific minor disappointment rather than real grief — missed the train, ran out of a favorite snack. Escalates to 大号泣 (daigoukyuu, exaggerated sobbing) or the meme-y ぴえん超えてぱおん (pien koete paon, 'beyond pien, into paon') for comic effect.
warningNotes: Skews young/feminine in who typically says it — a learner using it can read as trying too hard to sound trendy depending on who they are.

examples.text: 電車に間に合わなかった、ぴえん。
examples.reading: でんしゃにまにあわなかった、ぴえん。
examples.translation: I missed the train, ugh (sad face).

natural: 'I can't (handle this)' / 'no way' / 'I'm done' — used constantly as an exclamation, not just literally 'impossible'
whoUses: everyone informally, especially younger speakers, nationwide
usageContext: Extended far beyond its dictionary meaning into an all-purpose reaction to anything overwhelming, whether that's genuine distress, secondhand embarrassment, or being so charmed by something you can't cope (無理、かわいすぎる, 'I can't, this is too cute').

natural: your favorite idol, character, or celebrity — the one you actively support
whoUses: fandom communities broadly, now fully mainstream vocabulary across ages
usageContext: 推しを推す (oshi o osu, 'to support/promote your fave') describes actively cheering someone on — buying merch, voting, going to events. 推し活 (oshikatsu) is the broader lifestyle/hobby of supporting an oshi. Has moved well beyond niche fandom into everyday conversation.

examples.text: この人が私の推しです。
examples.reading: このひとがわたしのおしです。
examples.romaji: Kono hito ga watashi no oshi desu.
examples.translation: This is my favorite (the one I actively support).

natural: so good it feels almost holy — fandom hyperbole for something deeply satisfying to witness
usageContext: A dramatic escalation past 'cute' or 'great' — often used about a beloved fictional pairing or an idol's kind gesture, treating the moment as something to be almost reverently grateful for.

examples.text: 二人が笑い合ってるの、尊い。
examples.reading: ふたりがわらいあってるの、とうとい。
examples.translation: The two of them laughing together — it's too precious.

usageContext: Describes music, a place, or a mood that's laid-back in a good way — チルい曲 (chirui kyoku, 'a chill song'), 家でチルする (ie de chiru suru, 'to chill at home', with チル also used as a standalone verb-ish noun).

examples.text: この店、チルい雰囲気だね。
examples.reading: このみせ、チルいふんいきだね。
examples.romaji: Kono mise, chirui fun'iki da ne.
examples.translation: This place has a really chill vibe.

reading: ようキャ / いんキャ
literal: 'sun-type' / 'shadow-type' (陽 sun, 陰 shadow, + キャラ 'character')
natural: an outgoing, sociable type of person / a quiet, introverted type of person
usageContext: A blunt social-personality label pair, roughly 'popular/extroverted' vs. 'introverted/nerdy'. Can be neutral description or a light dig depending on tone — calling someone 陰キャ to their face can sting more than 陽キャ does.
warningNotes: 陰キャ in particular can land as an insult, not just a neutral label — read the room before using it about a real person.

examples.text: 彼は完全に陽キャだよね。
examples.reading: かれはかんぜんにようキャだよね。
examples.romaji: Kare wa kanzen ni youkya da yo ne.
examples.translation: He's totally the outgoing type, right?

natural: 'I'm doomed' / 'it's over' / no way out of this situation
whoUses: gamers originally, now broadly used casually across ages
usageContext: Extends the game-over sense to real life — oversleeping before an exam, realizing you've made an irreversible mistake. Usually said with dark humor rather than genuine despair.

examples.reading: しゅくだいわすれた、つんだ。
examples.translation: I forgot my homework, I'm doomed.

natural: 'god-tier' — used as a standalone exclamation of amazement, or as a prefix meaning 'amazing ___'
whoUses: everyone informally online, all ages by now
usageContext: 神対応 (kami-taiou, 'god-tier customer service/response') and 神回 (kamikai, 'a god-tier episode') are common compounds; 神！ alone as an exclamation praises anything from a meal to a stranger's kind gesture.

examples.text: 店員さんの神対応に感動した。
examples.reading: てんいんさんのかみたいおうにかんどうした。
examples.romaji: Ten'in-san no kami-taiou ni kandou shita.
examples.translation: I was moved by the staff member's incredible service.

whoUses: younger speakers, nationwide, both spoken and in texting
usageContext: A one-word agreement stronger and more current than plain そうだね — said the instant someone voices a complaint or opinion you share completely.

examples.text: — 月曜日って本当に憂鬱。 — それな。
examples.reading: — げつようびってほんとうにゆううつ。— それな。
examples.romaji: — Getsuyoubi tte hontou ni yuuutsu. — Sore na.

natural: lame / uncool / not up to par
whoUses: revived by younger speakers riding a Heisei-retro nostalgia wave, though the meaning has drifted
usageContext: An old word that came back into fashion with a shifted meaning — now closer to つまらない/ダサい ('boring/lame') than its original 'cowardly' sense. A good example of how slang recycles and mutates across generations rather than just being invented from nothing.

examples.text: そのオチ、シャバくない？
examples.reading: そのオチ、シャバくない？
examples.translation: Isn't that punchline kind of lame?

literal: nonsense compound: あざ(っす) [thanks, clipped] + まる [circle/cute suffix] + 水産 [fisheries, tacked on purely for absurd sound]
natural: a playful, exaggerated 'thanks so much!' — said for comic effect, not sincerity
whoUses: a legacy of 2010s gyaru-go (gal slang) wordplay, still recognized and occasionally revived as a joke
usageContext: Part of a genre of Japanese slang built by bolting nonsense syllables onto a real word purely because the result sounds fun to say — the 水産 ('fisheries') has zero logical connection to thanks, which is the entire joke.
warningNotes: Deliberately absurd and dated-on-purpose (used ironically/nostalgically now) — using it earnestly reads as a bit of a joke itself.

examples.text: 手伝ってくれてあざまる水産！
examples.reading: てつだってくれてあざまるすいさん！
examples.translation: Thanks so much for helping! (playfully over-the-top)
